narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 36 and an undergraduate GPA of 3.0, you've put together a respectable profile for law school admission, including plans to attend full-time and coming from a middle-income family background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 63.5%—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is well below the typical student's average of around 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers. This stood out as the strongest driver, far outweighing other elements in the decision. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward passing, since 36 is close to the dataset average of 36.7 and shows competitive standardized testing ability. Rounding out the top influences, your Year 1 decile of 2—again lower than the average of about 5.7—nudged the prediction further toward failure risk, highlighting early academic struggles relative to other students. Overall, while your LSAT provided a boost, the model weighed your consistently low decile rankings as the dominant signals of bar exam vulnerability, especially when compared to applicants who typically rank in the middle or higher deciles. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many in similar demographics succeed with stronger grade trajectories—but the data patterns the model learned from emphasized these academic deciles heavily. To move forward, focus on improving your academic standing, perhaps by retaking courses or pursuing a post-baccalaureate program to boost your deciles, and consider reapplying after demonstrating upward trends. You might also target schools with more holistic reviews or retake the LSAT to strengthen that positive factor even more. With targeted effort, you can shift these predictions in your favor for future applications.
